dmudrika Sdstra. It is held
by some that Sarnudra means Lord Visnu, while by others, Siva. It
may also be the name of a class of writers on this subject. It is the
name of a work quoted by Padmandbka. Vardha does not mention any
particular authority here except 'itfmcrrfr frfteT.' Even sage Garga
refers to Samudra* (LXVIII). Monier Williams mentions some
later works like the Sdmudmtilaka of Duriabhardjai Sdmvdrikakanlhd-
bharana and Sdmudrikacandrikd. The Viramilrodaya of MitramiSra (of
about the 14th century A.D.) mentions the signs of men and
women {Purufalakfana and Strilaksand) among a variety of subjects
included in this Sarhhitd. It quotes several works like the
and According to this work too the
Gargasarfihitd quotes Samudra on Anuka (heredity and previous life).
Garga is quoted in this work in respect of Mrjd or lustre. According
to the Bhavisyapurdm thick and large thumbs indicate lack of luck.
Garga, on the other hand, has given unusual importance to the eyes:
